stem: A hospital uses a TSH blood test to screen for hypothyroidism. The test has a sensitivity of 98% and a specificity of 92%. The prevalence of hypothyroidism in the patient population is estimated to be between 1.3% and 2.3%, with uncertainty due to regional variation and demographic differences in the hospital's catchment area. A patient receives a positive test result. What is the probability that the patient actually has hypothyroidism? Provide your answer as a probability with a confidence interval reflecting the uncertainty in prevalence.

ground_truth.answer: P(hypothyroidism | positive test) ranges from 0.139 to 0.224 as prevalence varies from 1.3% to 2.3%. Point estimate at 1.8% prevalence: 0.183.

solution_steps: Apply Bayes' theorem: P(D|+) = P(+|D)×P(D) / [P(+|D)×P(D) + P(+|¬D)×P(¬D)], where P(+|¬D) = 1-specificity = 0.08.
The confidence interval [0.139, 0.224] reflects the range of posterior probabilities over plausible prevalence values.
common_failure_modes: Confusing sensitivity (98%) with positive predictive value
Providing only a point estimate without acknowledging the prevalence uncertainty
Ignoring the base rate (prevalence) and using the test sensitivity as the answer
